content_en:
Riyadh al-Salihin (Gardens of the Righteous) by Imam Abu Zakariyya Yahya ibn Sharaf al-Nawawi (631–676 AH / 1233–1277 CE) is among the most widely read hadith compilations in the Islamic world, serving for centuries as an essential guide to the moral and spiritual life of the Muslim. Al-Nawawi opens the collection with the chapter on sincerity of intention — a choice that is itself a theological statement: all of what follows depends on the purity of the intention behind it.

The opening hadith of the entire collection is the foundational statement of Islamic ethics: 'Actions are judged only by intentions, and every person will have only what they intended. Whoever emigrates for the sake of Allah and His Messenger, their emigration will be for Allah and His Messenger. And whoever emigrates to obtain something of this world or to marry a woman, their emigration will be for whatever they emigrated for.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim, from Umar ibn al-Khattab.) Imam al-Nawawi follows the tradition of the great hadith scholars — including al-Shafi'i, who said this single hadith is a third of all Islamic knowledge — in opening his collection with this narration, signaling that intention is the internal reality that determines the spiritual value of every outward act.

The second hadith establishes the complementary truth: 'The reward of deeds depends upon the intentions, and every person will get the reward according to what he has intended.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) Al-Nawawi draws from these two narrations the principle that separates Islamic ethics from any merely legalistic system: Islam is not ultimately satisfied with the correct outward performance of deeds but demands that those deeds emerge from a correct inner orientation — the conscious dedication of the act to Allah alone.

Al-Nawawi then cites a series of Quranic verses that establish the Quranic foundation for this principle: 'And they were not commanded except to worship Allah, sincere to Him in religion' (Quran 98:5); 'Say: Indeed, my prayer, my sacrifice, my living, and my dying are for Allah, Lord of all worlds' (Quran 6:162); 'It is not their meat nor their blood that reaches Allah, but it is piety from you that reaches Him' (Quran 22:37). These verses together establish that Allah accepts from His servants not the physical act in isolation but the intention and piety that animates it.

The practical implication of this opening chapter is that every subsequent chapter of Riyadh al-Salihin — on repentance, patience, truthfulness, prayer, fasting, social relations, and all the rest — must be read through the lens of intention. A person who reads this book to accumulate knowledge for social prestige is not reading it in the spirit in which al-Nawawi compiled it. The 'gardens' of the righteous that the title evokes are gardens of the heart — places of inner cultivation that are watered by sincere intention and produce the fruits of genuine righteousness. Without the water of ikhlas (sincerity), the most beautiful garden will wither.
